item.title: Master Plan has protected Macau's ecological base and cultural values, says report
item.description:
The first periodic report of the Macao SAR Master Plan indicates that work to implement the document has made a “solid start”, substantiating the “prospectivity and viability” of the plan. This report also points out that the Master Plan has contributed to “simultaneously safeguarding the natural ecological base and the fundamental core cultural values ​​of Macau”.
The first periodic report of the Macao SAR Master Plan was published on Thursday, which came into force in February 2022. The document from the Directorate of Land and Urban Construction Services (DSSCU) points out that the implementation of the Master Plan has had a “solid start”. The report concludes that the work to execute the Master Plan within the scope of the implementation mechanism and continuous monitoring system, as well as the coordination between urban development and spatial use and distribution, “has made a solid start and is progressing systematically”, with the “various construction projects taking place in an orderly manner, substantiating the prospectivity and viability of the Master Plan”. “The results demonstrate that the construction of an urban framework for Macau, more regionally integrated, more habitable, more resilient, more dynamic and oriented towards quality of life, is gradually in the process of being implemented”, can be read in this first report on the Master Plan. This periodic report of the Master Plan indicates that, since its entry into force, “the implementation of the plan has registered progress in several areas, namely in the consolidation of the implementation mechanism and continuous monitoring system, in the articulation between urban development and the organization of spatial layout, in the optimization of the distribution and construction of equipment for collective use and in the promotion of habitable communities, as well as in the implementation of the improvement of the UOPG and their respective planning functions”. “Taken together, this progress reveals a consistent implementation trend and positive initial results”, points out the report, adding that, in the future, “it will be necessary to continue to actively articulate planning with new initiatives, with new plans, new missions and new developments”. The report highlights, for example, the area of ​​promoting construction projects. In the field of housing and urban renewal, public housing projects were completed in new urban areas such as Zone A of the New Urban Landfills and the temporary housing project in Areia Preta. At the same time, the construction project of buildings in the Iao Hon neighborhood is being promoted, “constituting a substantial step towards the construction of habitable communities”. Regarding equipment for collective use, the report highlights the conversion of the old Yat Yuen Canidrome into recreational and sports facilities and the works on the Islands Health Care Complex or the new Central Library. “These initiatives contribute to the provision of equipment necessary for daily life, including the respective leisure facilities, in order to increase the quality of the urban environment and the life of the population”, reads the DSSCU report. In transport and urban mobility, the report says that “people-oriented transport policies have been promoted, combined with the priority development strategy for public transport”, giving as examples the extension of the Light Rail network and the entry into operation of the Macau Bridge.
PRESERVATION OF THE HISTORICAL-CULTURAL ATMOSPHERE AND LANDSCAPE CHARACTERISTICS
In the field of environmental protection and safeguarding of cultural heritage, the report points out that measures were implemented such as the “strict protection of non-developable areas throughout the territory of Macau”, the “continuous conservation of the Cotai Ecological Zones”, the “creation of green and blue networks and the optimization of public spaces in each zone”, together with the entry into operation of the Macau World Heritage Monitoring Center and the promulgation of the “Safeguard and Management Plan for the Historic Center of Macau”. Macau”. These actions, says the report, “contribute to simultaneously safeguarding the natural ecological base and the fundamental core cultural values ​​of Macau, preserving the historical-cultural atmosphere and landscape characteristics, building and promoting a harmonious urban landscape between ‘mountain, sea and city'”. With regard to maritime areas, this report points out that “the safe advancement of the construction of maritime infrastructure facilities has contributed to promoting the development of the maritime economy and the effective use of maritime resources”.

item.title: Six biggest Chinese banks pay more than 427 billion yuan in dividends
item.description: The six largest Chinese state banks are expected to distribute more than 427 billion yuan in dividends for 2025, maintaining record levels, in a context of low interest rates. In total, combined payments from the Industrial and Commercial Bank of China, China Construction Bank, Agricultural Bank of China, Bank of China, Bank of Communications and Postal Savings Bank of China are expected to reach around 427.4 billion yuan, an increase of 1.6% compared to the previous year, according to calculations based on the institutions' profit distribution plans. ICBC and CCB lead the way, with dividends exceeding 100 billion yuan each. ICBC expects to distribute around 110.6 billion yuan, corresponding to a distribution ratio of 30% and the fifth consecutive year above that level. The CCB is expected to pay around 101.7 billion yuan, also with a ratio of 30%, extending the series to three years. ICBC vice-president and president, Liu Jun, stated that the bank will be able to dynamically adjust dividend policy and capital planning, depending on market conditions and investor demand. The Agricultural Bank of China and the Bank of China are expected to distribute, respectively, 87.3 billion and 72.9 billion yuan, both maintaining a ratio of 30%. The former recorded the fastest dividend growth among the six banks. The Bank of Communications and the Postal Savings Bank of China are expected to pay 28.7 billion and 26.2 billion yuan, respectively. The ability of these institutions to maintain high payments is based on stable revenue growth and robust capital levels. In 2025, the six banks recorded combined revenue of around 3.6 billion yuan, up 2.3% year-on-year, while net profit attributable to shareholders increased by 1.7% to 1.43 billion yuan. The consistent increase in shareholder remuneration occurs in a context in which regulators encourage listed companies to reinforce the frequency and predictability of dividends, at the same time as investors look for income-generating assets in an environment of low interest rates. Tier 1 core capital ratios remained solid, between approximately 10.5% and 14.6% at the end of last year, according to annual results, allowing distributions to continue. The average dividend yield of these six banks currently stands at around 4%, above the returns on bank deposits and most wealth management products. Citigroup anticipates that Chinese bank shares will outperform the market, pointing to defensive investment flows, potential state support and prospects for improved results. Lusa

item.title: Canteen for the elderly in Seac Pai Van scheduled for this month
item.description:
A cafeteria for the elderly will open this month in Seac Pai Van, offering meals for 20 patacas, after a Government subsidy. The project may be extended to other areas of Macau in the future. The area's Residents' Association expects a wider range of eligible beneficiaries for this canteen, while some traders fear business pressure. The Government confirmed the creation of a canteen for the elderly in Macau. The inauguration of the cafeteria will take place later this month in Seac Pai Van, being a pilot project that is part of the Executive's planning for a “15-minute circle of services for the elderly” on the islands. According to the initial plan of the Social Affairs and Culture department, the cafeteria will offer menus at a relatively low price. The estimated cost of a meal in the canteen for the elderly is around 35 patacas, with the Government subsidizing the difference of 15 patacas, which means that the selling price per meal will be 20 patacas. The authorities are working with partner entities to provide meals low in salt and fat, suitable for the elderly, offering options with different degrees of food consistency. With the experience of operating this canteen for the elderly, the project could be gradually expanded to other areas. The initiative was announced by O Lam, secretary for Social Affairs and Culture, last week in the Legislative Assembly. However, the exact location, as well as details of the operation of the cafeteria for the elderly, have not yet been revealed. BROAD RECIPIENTS
Regarding the opening of the cafeteria, the Seac Pai Van Residents' Association said it had received several requests for information from elderly people in the area, with the hope of implementing the plan as soon as possible. Cheong Meng Wai, quoted by Chinese-language Radio Macau, indicated that seniors want to know the details of the canteen, especially the mode of service, the reach of seniors eligible for the canteen, as well as the number of meals offered daily. The member of the Islands' Community Services Advisory Council asked, in this case, to also include people at least 60 years of age and people with disabilities in the canteen's service provision. Remember that, under the law in force in Macau, people aged 65 or over are considered elderly. "Will it also be possible to accept people aged 60 or over? What about isolated elderly people and families with an elderly couple? In Seac Pai Van there are many people who receive disability benefits, or who perhaps need social assistance, which are not always elderly people over 65. They may need the services provided by this canteen", he stated. Cheong Meng Wai said that there were also elderly people with reduced mobility suggesting home delivery of meals or collection of meals at the entrance of the building. He therefore asked for “diversified” services offered by the canteen to meet the needs of elderly residents. Seniors, on the other hand, want meals at even more affordable prices. Speaking to the newspaper Ou Mun, an elderly resident pointed out that she usually cooks at home because it is more economical, hoping that the price per meal can be reduced to ease the financial burden of elderly people without income. On the other hand, some traders said they were not aware that the Government would install a cafeteria for the elderly in the area, and are now afraid of a loss of business in the future. Noting that the commercial environment in Seac Pai Vai is “quite discouraging”, traders in public housing neighborhoods said that the majority of customers are elderly residents. “If the Government were to create a cafeteria, this would end up ‘stealing’ customers from us, further aggravating the operating pressure”, they state.

item.title: Macau remains a “key node for money laundering”, analysts say
item.description:
Organized crime experts consider that Macau continues to be a “key node for money laundering” by criminal organizations, despite the dismantling of the previous VIP gaming system in the territory
According to Martin Pubrick, a former member of the Hong Kong Royal Police and an expert on corruption and organized crime, although Beijing has dismantled the system of VIP gambling solicitors, known as 'junkets', that once dominated the city's casino sector, Macau continues to be a crucial gateway for money laundering in the region. “Although large Chinese criminal syndicates have moved operations across Southeast Asia in response to repressive measures, Macau continues to be an operational and meeting point for these deeply rooted networks,” he said. The junket system allowed “huge capital flows from mainland China” and enriched groups linked to the triads, a “process that ended in the years before the Covid-19 pandemic, when the central government expanded the anti-corruption campaign, strengthened capital controls and prosecuted several junkets in Macau to send a clear message”, explained the analyst. Alvin Chau, former head of the city's largest junket group, the Suncity Group, was arrested in 2021 and later sentenced to 18 years in prison for criminal association and exploitation of illicit gambling. In a parallel process, Levo Chan, leader of the Tak Chun Group, the second largest junket in Macau, was sentenced to 13 years in prison on similar charges. John Wojcik, senior researcher at Infoblox Threat Intelligence and former analyst at the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime, makes the same analysis. Although the message from local authorities is clear that “the old ways of business are over”, Macau maintains strategic importance in this parallel industry, he said. The business model of Macau's junkets and associated criminal groups was, until 2019, centered on casinos. Today, however, activity has shifted to illegal online betting and gaming, with cryptocurrency payments and a significant expansion into large-scale digital fraud schemes, Pubrick noted. “Currency exchange offices, pawn shops and transactions via credit cards have absorbed this demand, which could mean that money laundering in Macau is less centralized and less visible today,” noted the analyst. But “Macau continues to be a key node for money laundering”, he reiterated. These criminal groups, Pubrick described, have adapted, moving operations to jurisdictions with weaker regulations, such as Cambodia, the Philippines, which was home to several offshore online gambling companies, as well as the border areas of Myanmar. Many migrated to “illegal online betting, digital fraud and cryptocurrency transactions”, creating a “geographically distributed” business, with victims today expanding to Europe and the Americas. “Macau is no longer the center of money laundering, but it is a regulated gateway that continues to be exploited by criminal groups from Mainland China, Hong Kong and Taiwan”, reinforced Pubrick. Two recent cases illustrate this reality. In March, Taiwan authorities arrested 20 people involved in a money laundering scheme estimated to cost 33 billion Taiwan dollars (890 million euros). Among the two dozen detainees, 10 were accused by the Taiwan Public Prosecutor's Office for, in particular, using Macau casinos to launder money from illegal gambling on the Internet. “Money mules” inflated credit limits, purchased large quantities of tokens and exchanged them for local currency, disguising the funds. Two alleged leaders of the scheme remain on the run. In a second case, Lin Ping-wen, a Taiwanese fugitive linked to one of the biggest illegal gambling scandals on Formosa Island and wanted for illegal gambling and money laundering, was shot dead in Cambodia, in Sihanoukville, a coastal city in the southeast of the country known for its casinos. With a background in organized crime in Taipei, Lin was also linked to a baseball match fixing scandal in 2007 and later joined the VIP gambling promotion sector in Macau, with links to the Suncity Group. Lusa

item.title: Aguiar-Branco visits Macau on Friday and Saturday
item.description: José Pedro Aguiar-Branco, president of the Assembly of the Republic (AR) of Portugal, begins an official visit to China today, also visiting Macau on Friday and Saturday. In Macao, Aguiar-Branco will meet Sam Hou Fai and André Cheong, for example. The President of the Assembly of the Republic (AR) of Portugal begins a visit to China today, also visiting Macau and Hong Kong. José Pedro Aguiar-Branco will be in Macao between Friday and Saturday. According to a note from José Pedro Aguiar-Branco's office, this trip took place at the invitation of the president of the Standing Committee of the National People's Assembly of the People's Republic of China, Zhao Leji, made during his visit to Portugal in November 2024 and is part of the memorandum of understanding signed in July 2017 by the presidents of the two parliaments at the time. On Friday, Aguiar-Branco will visit Portugal's exhibitor at the International Tourism Exhibition (MITE) and will have meetings with the Chief Executive of the Macao SAR, Sam Hou Fai, as well as with the president of the territory's Legislative Assembly, André Cheong. On the same day, the AR president will go to the Portuguese School of Macau (EPM). On Saturday, you will visit some emblematic places of the Portuguese presence in the region, such as the Santa Casa da Misericórdia, the Ruins of São Paulo, the Portuguese Bookstore and the Consulate General of Portugal in Macau and Hong Kong. The president of the AR will also have a meeting with the Portuguese legal community in Macau and will be present at the inauguration of an exhibition commemorating the 25th of April, entitled “The arts are on the Street”, at the Consulate. On Saturday afternoon, Aguiar-Branco and the Portuguese delegation will travel to Hong Kong to see the exhibition “Estórias lusas”, about the Portuguese presence in this territory. They will also meet with members of the Portuguese community living in the HKSAR. Today, in Beijing, Aguiar-Branco is scheduled to meet with the vice-president of the People's Republic of China, Han Zheng, and with the president of the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress of the People's Republic of China, Zhao Leji, followed by an official dinner. Tomorrow, in Shanghai, the president of the AR will participate in a lunch with members of the Portuguese community, visit the Fosun Art Center, an urban planning exhibition center and meet with local authorities and the president of the Standing Committee of the National People's Congress of this municipality, Huang Lixin. The last official visit of a President of the Assembly of the Republic to China took place in 2018 (when Ferro Rodrigues held the position) and the Portuguese delegation will include deputies from the Portugal-China Parliamentary Friendship Group from various parties: Hugo Carneiro (PSD), Paulo Núncio (CDS-PP), Edite Estrela (PS), Felicidade Vital (Chega) and Paula Santos (PCP).

item.title: Restaurant sector with pessimistic business outlook for this year
item.description:
A survey on local restaurants reveals that the sector generally had a drop in business last year and remains pessimistic about this year's activities. According to the Macau Restaurant and Beverage Owners Association, more than 60% of traders surveyed said that holding major events did not contribute to any positive impact on business volume.
Macau's restaurant sector continues to suffer impacts on its business, with more than 60% of owners, interviewed in a survey, stating that they registered a decline in business volume last year, with an average drop of 10% to 30% compared to 2024. According to the survey carried out by the Union of Associations of Restaurant and Beverage Establishment Owners of Macau, 25% of respondents reported that the business remained “stable” as in the previous year, while 15% indicated that it had an average growth of 10% to 20%. Regarding the operating prospects for this year, more than a third of the traders surveyed stated that they were “pessimistic” and 11% admitted that they were “quite pessimistic”. Only 2% of respondents said they were “very optimistic”, and 15% said they were “optimistic”. 25% of respondents believe that this year's commercial situation will be the same as last year. According to the association's report, currently led by former deputy Chan Chak Mo, by February 2026, there were more than seven thousand food and beverage establishments in Macau, including restaurants, take-away shops and food court stalls, among others. The organization of this survey, however, did not reveal the number of traders who responded to the questionnaire, but highlighted that, of the respondents, 68% were spaces located in residential neighborhoods, and 32% in tourist areas of Macau. The weakening of purchasing power and the fact that more and more residents choose to consume outside Macau are considered the main reasons for the drop in business for local restaurants. The Association also highlighted that the closure of satellite casinos in the NAPE and ZAPE areas “severely affected” the operational situation of nearby restaurant establishments. The Government has been organizing promotional campaigns aimed at encouraging consumption in certain areas, such as NAPE, ZAPE and non-tourist areas. However, the results of the survey indicate that 62% of traders state that such measures “did not have any positive impact on turnover”. For owners of food and beverage establishments, the operational pressures faced are, in this order, labor costs, store rents, costs of raw materials, fuel, water and electricity, and expenses paid to 'take-away' platforms that help receive orders for food pickup or home delivery. With regard to the overall prices of dishes, compared to 2024, 71% of the establishments surveyed indicated that they had not changed prices, while 9% reported having increased prices compared to last year and 20% indicated that they had lowered them. The survey also showed that 94% of the food and beverage establishments interviewed confirmed that they will adopt measures to improve business performance, including launching promotional offers (24%) such as immediate discount coupons and promotional menus; launch “innovative products” that satisfy market needs (19%); expand the online market (18%); launch own brand products (13%), among others. In its report, the Union of Associations of Owners of Food and Beverage Establishments of Macau presented several suggestions to improve the development of the sector. Among them, the association proposed launching free half-day tours for international tourists who stopover in Macau, and optimizing the operation of the terraces in terms of aesthetics and cleanliness.

item.title: CURB brings bike tours to Coloane for the first time
item.description: The Center for Architecture and Urbanism (CURB) association presents the eighth edition of its urban exploration bike tour project, On The Move, now titled OTM VIII: Coloane Lookout. For the first time, the initiative leaves the Macau Peninsula and invites participants to explore the landscapes, rhythms and daily life of the island of Coloane. The event takes place on May 2nd, departing from Coloane Pier, and offers four sessions spread over two different routes. After seven editions dedicated to discovering the Macau Peninsula, CURB's On The Move project now decides to venture beyond the bridges. The eighth edition, entitled Coloane Lookout, takes participants to cycle along the lesser-known paths of the quieter part of the island in the south of the territory, in an exercise that combines physical movement, attentive listening and collective artistic production. The initiative, financed by the Fund for the Development of Culture, is scheduled for May 2nd, with a meeting point at Coloane Pier. The organization prepared two different routes, repeated in two sessions each: the Town Route, dedicated to the streets and alleys of the town, with departures at 10:00 am and 4:00 pm; and the Field Route, which extends through more open and scenic areas, with sessions at 11:30 am and 2:30 pm. Each group will last one hour and will be limited to ten participants, with the sessions guided in English by Nuno Soares, president of CURB. The requirements to participate are simple: be over 18 years old, know how to ride a bike and register using the form available on the association's social networks, on a first-come, first-serve system confirmed by email. The organization provides all the necessary equipment, including helmets, reflective vests and headphones to follow the explanations along the route. Over the years, On The Move has established itself as an alternative way of experiencing the city. In previous editions, the tours covered neighborhoods such as Patane, San Kio, Fai Chi Kei and Toi San, always with the aim of revealing less visible layers of the territory. In older editions, the event also took place at nightfall, with routes designed to capture the urban lighting and nighttime atmosphere of Macau. For this debut, CURB intends to capture sounds and images along the routes, material that will later be integrated into the creation of video art and a documentary. This audiovisual piece will be shown in a parallel session at Ponte 9 – Plataforma Criativa, on a date yet to be announced, following the model already used in previous editions. Coloane, with its more winding geography, beaches, hidden trails and heritage of ancient fishing and farming communities, offers a significant contrast to the urban density of the peninsula. Touring Coloane by bike will allow a slower and more sensorial approach to a part of the city that is not always seen by the inhabitants of the center. A change in the way we listen to the environmental sounds of nature, feel the textures of old walls and unexpected perspectives on the landscape. Unrelated to the event, but relevant for those traveling from the Peninsula, there is a transport option that adds value to the experience. The ferry route between Cais da Barra and Cais de Coloane makes the crossing in around 25 minutes. During the sea journey, passengers can successively enjoy the horizons of Macau, Cotai, Hengqin and Coloane, including passing through mangrove areas and the groups of herons that live there. Departures from Cais da Barra to Coloane take place between 10:30 am and 6:00 pm, and the return between 11:00 am and 6:30 pm, with a cost of 60 patacas per trip. This maritime connection, which uses Cais da Barra as a starting point, brings the CURB headquarters, located at Ponte 9, closer to the event meeting point in Coloane. CURB, founded in 2014, is a non-profit institution that promotes research, education and dissemination of knowledge in the areas of architecture, urbanism and urban culture. The association acts as an exchange platform between academia, civil society, professionals and government institutions, and is also responsible for organizing Open House Macau, the first of its kind in Asia.

item.title: Burmese military junta leader accused of genocide in Indonesia
item.description: Two survivors of the Rohingya Muslim minority have filed a genocide complaint in Indonesia against the head of the Burmese military junta, Min Aung Hlaing, the third accusation of its kind. Yasmin Ullah and Noor Azizah, two Rohingya women who fled Myanmar following the 2017 military campaign against the ethnic group, filed the complaint with the Jakarta Public Prosecutor's Office, accompanied by international lawyers and other Indonesian civil society leaders, according to a statement posted on social media by the Rohingya organization Maìyifuìnor. "Nobody chooses to be a refugee, nobody chooses to survive a genocide. If I could, I would like to be at home and live in peace with the rest of the people of Myanmar (…). That's why I'm here, because my people can't," said Noor Azizah during the hearing, according to a video published on her profile on the social network Instagram. The accusation against Min Aung Hlaing, who was appointed president of Myanmar on Friday by a military-dominated parliament, is based on the principle of universal jurisdiction, which allows courts to judge international crimes, the organization Maiyafuinor highlighted on the social network Facebook. Min Aung Hlaing, who led the coup d'état in February 2021, was the commander-in-chief of the Army when the Burmese Armed Forces launched an operation in August 2017 against the Rohingya population in northern Rakhine State, in the east of the country. The military operation, launched after an attack by Rohingya rebels, caused the exodus of more than 720,000 refugees to neighboring Bangladesh, where thousands of people expelled in previous years already lived in exile. The UN estimates that, during this campaign, which it classified as “ethnic cleansing with characteristics of genocide”, at least 10,000 people were killed, while hundreds of villages were devastated by arson. There are currently two international arrest warrants against the coup leader, issued in 2024 by the Office of the Prosecutor of the International Criminal Court (ICC) and by a court in Buenos Aires. Both seek the extradition of the Burmese military officer for his alleged involvement in the Rohingya genocide. Every year, thousands of Rohingya risk their lives on dangerous sea journeys to reach Indonesia, the country with the largest Muslim population in the world, where a large community of this ethnic group resides, not recognized by Myanmar. Lusa
